solution.p: We will factor r^{2}+13r+36 where a=1, b=13, and c=36.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For r^{2}+13r+36, since the product of 4 and 9 is 36, and the sum of 4 and 9 is 13, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+13r+36\amp =\left(r+4\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+9\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (36) = 36.

The number 36 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(36)\amp -1(-36)\amp 2(18)\amp -2(-18)
ewline3(12)\amp -3(-12)\amp 4(9)\amp -4(-9)
ewline6(6)\amp -6(-6)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 13, and this sum happens with 9+4=13 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+13r+36\amp =r^{2}+9r+4r+36
ewline \amp =r\mathopen{}\left(r+9ight)+4\mathopen{}\left(r+9ight)
ewline \amp =\left(r+4ight)\mathopen{}\left(r+9ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in r^{2}+13r+36 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}

The third term, 36, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(36)\amp -1(-36)\amp 2(18)\amp -2(-18)\newline3(12)\amp -3(-12)\amp 4(9)\amp -4(-9)\newline6(6)\amp -6(-6)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with r^{2}+13r+36 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+13r+36\amp =\left(r+4\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+9\right)\end{aligned}
